paragraphs.body: Have you ever heard of the Steelers? The answer is probably yes. But have you ever wondered why the Steelers are called the Steelers? This was a question that always came to me when I was a kid. While the answer to that question is relatively straightforward, the team is named for Pittsburgh’s former steelworks renowned, the impact this has on the city today and me growing up here isn’t as straightforward.

paragraphs.body: In the article, “Beyond the politics of nostalgia: What the fall of the steel industry can tell us about the future of America,” Tony Dutzik asserts that the United States has passed its golden age of manufacturing, and that political promises to bring it back are misleading. Reminiscing on the economic prosperity that American manufacturing once had leaves out the environmental damage and pollution it brought to American cities like Pittsburgh. Instead of focusing on the nostalgia of past manufacturing, American cities should follow in the steps of Pittsburgh and invest in green industries and postindustrial options. He supports his point by developing several important ideas. First, Dutzik argues that the cause of the manufacturing collapse in America was due to the slowdown in demand for steel, new union contracts increasing the cost to produce in the U.S., and other countries developing their own, cheaper steel industries. Next, he highlights how promises to return America back to a manufacturing country are fake choices. Promises from the left and the right to recreate industrial jobs are not sustainable because of cheaper alternative options abroad. Finally, Dutzik believes that to work towards postindustrial investment and projects, America must abandon past concepts of economic development and prosperity. Pittsburgh represents this concept as a city that lost many of its manufacturing jobs; it has recovered somewhat by post-industrial industries, which have also transformed it into a greener and cleaner city.

annotations.text: In the article, “Beyond the politics of nostalgia: What the fall of the steel industry can tell us about the future of America,” Tony Dutzik asserts that the United States has passed its golden age of manufacturing, and that political promises to bring it back are misleading.

Reminiscing on the economic prosperity that American manufacturing once had leaves out the environmental damage and pollution it brought to American cities like Pittsburgh. Instead of focusing on the nostalgia of past manufacturing, American cities should follow in the steps of Pittsburgh and invest in green industries and postindustrial options.

paragraphs.body: When I talk to many of my old high school friends that have moved out of the city for college or for work, or even when I talk to people still living in the city. They express a certain dislike for the city. This always surprises me, because as someone born and raised here, I’ve always been fond of it and never felt like I needed to escape. My first thought is to ask why. What is it specifically about the city that turns people away? Many people dislike the “bad” weather, the steep hills, or, most commonly, the small size compared to other major U.S. cities. But for me, growing up here has made me appreciate the unique geography, numerous green industries, rich culture and close-knit communities.

annotations.text: But for me, growing up here has made me appreciate the unique geography, numerous green industries, rich culture and close-knit communities.

paragraphs.body: As the article iterates, Pittsburgh isn’t what it used to be, but I don’t take that to have a negative connotation. It certainly isn’t the manufacturing giant that it used to be, as the city population and job opportunities have declined. In the article, Dutzik himself writes, “American steel mills now produce roughly the same amount of steel with only half as many workers as they did in 1990 – and many, many times fewer workers than they did during Pittsburgh’s industrial heyday.” In other words, Dutzik believes that while it may seem like Pittsburgh and the U.S. are producing the same amount of steel, the steel industry has shrunk dramatically from what it once was. Manufacturing jobs that once employed thousands of people in Western Pennsylvania are no longer available. Although, the steel industry that once defined Pittsburgh brought the city more than just manufacturing jobs and the steel city culture.

paragraphs.body: An area in the East End of Pittsburgh I’ve driven past or visited countless times over the years is Bakery Square. Standing in that area, there are dozens of modern glass buildings with offices for tech giant Google, electronic manufacturer Philips, along with many others. As I’ve grown up, it seems that every time I visit, the area gets bigger, with more restaurants, stores, and companies popping up. What is remarkable to me, though, is that my parents would always tell me that before all the new companies moved in there, all that stood there was a Nabisco Cookies factory. My mom tells me she misses when she could smell the fresh-baked cookies when driving past the factory. While I never experienced that, I have experienced the staggering growth of the old cookie factory into a bustling center of shops, restaurants, offices, and much more.

annotations.text: What is remarkable to me, though, is that my parents would always tell me that before all the new companies moved in there, all that stood there was a Nabisco Cookies factory. My mom tells me she misses when she could smell the fresh-baked cookies when driving past the factory.

paragraphs.body: The other category that Pittsburgh used to lead in along with steel production was pollution. Dutzik agrees when he writes, “The other thing that Pittsburgh was most known for in the 20th century, besides steel, was pollution.” Dutzik's point is that people who long for the return of steel manufacturing forget the immense dangerous pollutants that came with it. This pollution has had far-reaching impacts on people's health, and signs of this dangerous past can still be found all over the city.

annotations.text: The other category that Pittsburgh used to lead in along with steel production was pollution.

paragraphs.body: Only a couple of blocks from my house growing up, there is a small, old church with a distinct black stone exterior. While there were no longer services held there, it was used as a community center for my neighborhood. I had passed the building almost every day and had been inside numerous times, whether it was for pottery class, community events, or youth group services. I had many fond childhood memories made there, and any time I was in that building, there was sure to be something exciting or fun happening.

annotations.text: While there were no longer services held there, it was used as a community center for my neighborhood. I had passed the building almost every day and had been inside numerous times, whether it was for pottery class, community events, or youth group services.

paragraphs.body: Traveling past this building and going inside for events throughout my childhood, it never occurred to me why this building had a dark black stone color. When I was 9 or 10, I remember the building closed for renovation, so the different events I would attend had to move location. Crews began to come and clean the exterior, which revealed a shocking, bright, and beautiful beige exterior, which was the original color of the stone. The steel city's history of smoke and smog had permanently discolored the church. This is one of my favorite facts about Pittsburgh’s unique history. My favorite part, though, is that the old church was used as a place for community engagement before and after.

annotations.text: The steel city's history of smoke and smog had permanently discolored the church.

paragraphs.body: While it is true that the city is significantly smaller than its peak steel-producing powerhouse days, as someone born and raised here, the city has recovered and is more beautiful and vibrant than ever before. There are blossoming industries in technology, healthcare, and education. As well as a well-connected community and unique characteristics that you won’t find in any other city. This is why you shouldn’t overlook or disregard Pittsburgh, as someone who was born and raised and loves this city.
